quotes.quote: The problem wasn’t the market around the products we were building; the problem was the product itself.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues the modern data stack didn’t fail because the market turned; it failed because the product never converged on a shared, vivid vision of what the data experience should be. The early, clear goal of modernizing legacy tools drifted into vague promises of “data democratization,” leaving vendors building disjointed pieces without a Schelling point, so the ecosystem fragmented and stalled.
quotes.key_insight: Without a shared, tangible vision of the end-user experience, the modern data stack splintered into disconnected tools despite ample capital and momentum.

quotes.quote: Data helps on the edges.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data rarely transforms big, high-leverage decisions; it mostly nudges outcomes at the margins. The real advantage comes from finding small edges that can be repeated frequently, or better yet, using data to discover what you’re already slightly better at and then doing that more often so the edge compounds.
quotes.key_insight: Data’s real power is not in heroic, one-off decisions but in identifying small, repeatable edges and multiplying them through frequency.

quotes.quote: Don’t be intuition-driven or data-driven; be quantity-driven, and look exclusively for places where you can create a durable advantage through frequent, small—and in the aggregate, predictable—bets that you’ve tilted in your favor.

quotes.quote: Roulette is one giant dice roll.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that most dashboard-driven analysis is closer to roulette than science: we see patterns and invent narratives in essentially noisy data. He praises Cedric Chin’s simple, iterative method for reading operational data because it gives permission to ignore false complexity and focus on actionable variation. The post then pivots to influencer culture, framing brand value as a social association game where the “wrong” endorsers can erode or even perversely strengthen a brand.
quotes.key_insight: Good analysis comes less from sophisticated techniques than from disciplined, simple observation of meaningful variation—and social perception games in branding operate by similarly irrational but powerful pattern‑seeking dynamics.

quotes.quote: Most for-profit companies have two sets of values.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that corporate values are inherently vague and often serve as flexible, quasi-legal tools that can be weaponized by management or disputed by employees. The real issue isn’t that companies want values, but that these slogans obscure tradeoffs between profit and principle, creating ambiguity and conflict. He proposes replacing abstract values with concrete precedents that show how a company actually behaves under pressure.
quotes.key_insight: Corporate values are less useful as slogans than as documented precedents that reveal how a company actually balances profit and principle in real situations.

quotes.quote: The party was the point.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack boom was driven as much by hype and attention-seeking as by genuine technical conviction, and that the post-ChatGPT shift to AI has hollowed out that “club.” The slowdown creates a better moment for durable startups to rebuild proven ideas more deliberately, while he also critiques the industry’s near-religious faith that “data” is inherently valuable.
quotes.key_insight: The data-stack hype cycle is fading, and the real opportunity now is in rebuilding established ideas thoughtfully while questioning the industry’s faith that data is inherently valuable.

quotes.quote: The bigger problem, however, was about trust.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Carta’s doomed exchange (CartaX) wasn’t fundamentally different from Google’s data-driven ad marketplace, but it violated privacy expectations by making the human, personal act of outreach visible. The real failure was optics: people accept algorithmic data brokering at scale, yet recoil when a person appears to rifle through private records. He then generalizes to startup strategy, warning that “step two” visions often collapse because success at step one hardens a company’s identity and constraints.
quotes.key_insight: Carta’s exchange collapsed less because its model was unique or unethical in kind, and more because the human-facing optics of data use shattered trust that algorithmic marketplaces usually avoid.

quotes.quote: The difference between Google and Carta may not be so much in how they manage their exchanges, but in how they manage how their appearances.

quotes.key_insight: Carta’s exchange collapsed less because its model was unique or unethical in kind, and more because the human-facing optics of data use shattered trust that algorithmic marketplaces usually avoid.

quotes.quote: If you want to build an exchange, build an exchange.

The real failure was optics: people accept algorithmic data brokering at scale, yet recoil when a person appears to rifle through private records. He then generalizes to startup strategy, warning that “step two” visions often collapse because success at step one hardens a company’s identity and constraints.
quotes.key_insight: Carta’s exchange collapsed less because its model was unique or unethical in kind, and more because the human-facing optics of data use shattered trust that algorithmic marketplaces usually avoid.

quotes.quote: In Silicon Valley startups’ employees and investors, this is the whole scheme: Not to build a business that steadily makes a little bit of money for a long time, but to build a business that someone—another company, a private equity firm, public investors on the stock market—buys for a lot of money all at once.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley startups operate on a “scheme” where equity is valued by the next buyer rather than by steady profits, and that this dynamic shapes incentives from hiring to executive comp. The Alteryx sale illustrates the scheme faltering under higher rates and stricter markets, pushing companies toward a capital-allocator model that prioritizes cash flow over hype. He closes by skewering a viral Twitter “funding” lie as a quasi‑fraud enabled by social media’s incentive structure.
quotes.key_insight: When the venture “scheme” of selling future potential gets harder to run, companies like Alteryx are revalued as cash-generating assets, shifting power to capital allocators and exposing the incentives that built the startup model.

quotes.quote: People who are good at Googling aren’t good because they know that you search for keywords in URLs by using the “allinurl:” prefix; they’re good because they have an instinct for how Google works.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that ChatGPT, like Google before it, rewards intuition and practice more than formal tricks, and that its real value today is speeding up tedious data work rather than generating novel analytical insight. Using Seth Stephens‑Davidowitz’s 30‑day AI‑assisted book as a case study, he says AI will flood us with faster, shakier analyses, making credibility and narrative framing even more decisive than the math itself.
quotes.key_insight: AI speeds analysis but doesn’t solve judgment, so credibility and narrative cues will matter even more as conclusions become cheaper to produce.

quotes.quote: In the hierarchy of things you want to sell, databases aren’t actually the cash cow, but the middle fish—bigger than the apps, but smaller than the cloud.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Microsoft’s Fabric and Databricks’ MosaicML deal show the modern data stack is too small to be the real battleground; the bigger fight is for the next “higher-order bit” in AI infrastructure. Databases and MDS tools can’t win by eating smaller apps because cloud providers still sit above them, so vendors are repositioning to become AI model infrastructure providers where access to data is the key advantage.
quotes.key_insight: The modern data stack’s consolidation is a sideshow; the real strategic move is building AI infrastructure where data-rich vendors can climb above the database layer.

quotes.quote: If database vendors want to build durable defenses against their larger predators, eating the modern data stack doesn’t help all that much.

quotes.quote: In other words, data hasn’t been put on a corporate pedestal because its contents are more valuable than the contents of customer interviews, or support tickets, or online reviews.
quotes.tone: analytical and critical, with a reflective edge
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the real power of data is not its objectivity but its aggregatability, which makes structured numbers easy to summarize while rich qualitative information remains costly to synthesize. He contends that LLMs can act as an “aggregate function” for text, unlocking the value of unstructured data that traditional data stacks ignore. The future of data platforms, he suggests, is less about squeezing more out of structured data and more about making qualitative corpora computable.
quotes.key_insight: The real breakthrough in data platforms will come from using LLMs to aggregate and analyze unstructured text, not from further optimizing traditional structured data pipelines.

quotes.quote: In 2021, investors were willing to pay premiums for looser, more atmospheric, more gameable, and less monetizable signs of progress.
quotes.summary: Benn argues that 2021-era data startup valuations were inflated by stacked multipliers—market exuberance, community hype, and category narratives—that have since collapsed as rates rose and sentiment cooled. He says many companies can’t “grow into” those valuations, so the most plausible path is opportunistic fundraising that preserves the signal of success even if the market fundamentals are weaker.
quotes.key_insight: The post’s core insight is that 2021’s stacked valuation multipliers for data startups have unwound, so the only way to sustain lofty prices is to find a single bidder willing to buy a narrative.

quotes.quote: It’s all handstands again—months of daily practice, and not a two-week sprint.

He then contrasts BI tools with spreadsheets, claiming Excel’s static, file-based nature is its moat, and closes with a satirical take on privatizing productivity gains inside companies.
quotes.key_insight: Most AI add-ons will decay without sustained, non-glamorous engineering effort, just as past tech hypes did, while Excel’s very limitations are what keep it irreplaceable.

The only enduring moat is sustained effort: you can hack attention or early growth, but lasting advantage comes from doing the boring, direct, head‑to‑head work better than incumbents.
quotes.key_insight: Startups feel surprising hard because founders bet on clever hacks, but real, durable success comes only from sustained, unglamorous execution.

quotes.quote: Both Israelis and Palestinians are crushed right now.
quotes.summary: The post assembles firsthand accounts from people in Israel and Gaza to show how the war is devastating daily life, safety, and work across both communities. It argues that beyond the headlines, the human cost is vast and intimate, and that the least we can do is acknowledge the tragedy and offer practical support.
quotes.key_insight: By centering personal testimonies and concrete ways to help, the post frames the war as a shared human tragedy that demands empathy and practical support, not silence.

quotes.quote: There were no AI-powered features.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses Coalesce in San Diego as a lens to argue that the data industry is maturing away from hype and toward process-heavy, often boring work, with AI notably absent. That shift strips away the “main-character” career narrative and forces practitioners to ask whether they’re in the field for the craft or for the promised spotlight. He hopes the community keeps making room for human celebration even as the industry professionalizes and attention moves on.
quotes.key_insight: As data work shifts from hype to legwork, the industry must decide whether it values the craft itself or merely the status it once promised.

quotes.quote: The problems that data teams deal with aren’t likely to be solved by some fancy flourish of AI, or by a wrapper around ChatGPT.

quotes.quote: Venture capital doesn’t ruin products—it ruins this myth.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the popular story that venture capital “ruins” good products is incomplete and often misattributes cause. The real degradation comes not from the cash itself but from the growth imperative that VC financing imposes, which pushes companies to expand features and markets beyond a product’s natural fit.
quotes.key_insight: VC funding doesn’t inherently worsen products; the need to sustain rapid growth does, by forcing expansion beyond a product’s natural scope.

quotes.quote: The problem with the narrative that VC money ruins products is that it has to ruin them through some mechanism.

quotes.quote: The dance isn’t a ballet; it’s a mosh pit.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that there is a persistent gap between what data tools can do and what teams actually use, much like glossy Office “vision” videos versus real messy workflows. The problem is less about missing features and more about adoption, skepticism, and the lack of belief that investing in a tool’s full potential will pay off.
quotes.key_insight: The hardest problem for modern data tools is not capability but belief-driven adoption—teams rarely invest enough to realize the tools’ full potential.

quotes.quote: Though teams could solve lots of their problems with Y42, very few will.

quotes.quote: But it takes much longer, happens much later, and is a much more fitful and distracting journey than almost every startup thinks that it is.
quotes.summary: The post argues that the common startup plan to win SMBs first and then graduate to enterprise rarely works as imagined, because enterprise sales take far longer, demand different products and buying motions, and can derail a company’s model. Stancil says most startups either need a path to scale within their initial buyer segment or start as enterprise-first from day one, rather than assuming a smooth transition later.
quotes.key_insight: Assuming you can seamlessly pivot from SMB traction to enterprise revenue is a strategic error; either your initial segment must scale to $100M or you must build for enterprise from the start.

quotes.quote: In the best cases, interminably flirting with the enterprise is psychological torture.

quotes.quote: Ok, look. It’s 2023. If our issue is a skill shortage, it’s not an issue. It’s an excuse.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s “purple” era—where analytics people bridge business and engineering through bespoke insight-hunting—has not delivered reliable value, despite better tools and more talent. He suggests the real maturation path is toward standardized, accountant-like practices with shared metrics and rules (e.g., SOMA) that trade novelty for trust and institutional reliability.
quotes.key_insight: The data profession will deliver more durable value by standardizing metrics and practices like accountants do, rather than chasing bespoke insights and perpetuating the myth that talent or tools are the main bottleneck.

quotes.quote: But for a job to be mainstream, it has to be able to hire mainstream talent. So if we still think our problem is that the talent we have can’t do the job we want them to do, the problem isn’t them; the problem is the job.

quotes.quote: To create true institutional trust around the work that data teams do, we might need to do what accountants do: Give our work some rules.

quotes.quote: Counterintuitively, we’d go further if we didn’t think of ourselves as intrepid explorers searching for novelty, but embraced our role as accountants—reliable, pedantic, and, actually, less boring than us.

quotes.quote: People stopped looking for specialized tools to solve every problem, and instead started trying to stretch the ones they have across several categories at once.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is consolidating not just in vendor count but in category count: buyers are compressing spending and pushing tools to become broader, more BI-like platforms. As a result, specialized tools either get absorbed, stagnate as niche players, or rebrand into wider enterprise suites, and $10M ARR or “category leader” status no longer signals inevitability.
quotes.key_insight: Category boundaries in the data stack are collapsing under buyer-driven consolidation, making breadth and enterprise fit more important than niche differentiation or early revenue milestones.

quotes.quote: Software categories aren’t defined by Gartner, or VCs, or marketing taglines; they’re defined by buyers.

quotes.quote: My unrelenting belief is that every BI-adjacent product is likely bound for one of three destinations: Be subsumed into some bigger and unabashed BI platform; stall, and become a niche player serving a small market; or get acquired by a major SaaS business that wants to start selling better reporting.

As a result, specialized tools either get absorbed, stagnate as niche players, or rebrand into wider enterprise suites, and $10M ARR or “category leader” status no longer signals inevitability.
quotes.key_insight: Category boundaries in the data stack are collapsing under buyer-driven consolidation, making breadth and enterprise fit more important than niche differentiation or early revenue milestones.

quotes.quote: In the data world, I don’t think that’s true anymore. The market is big enough, fragmented enough, and disparate enough that getting to $10 million simply doesn’t—or didn’t, at least—have that much signal anymore.

quotes.quote: The greatest trick that VCs ever pulled is convincing startups that down rounds are a disaster.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the stigma around down rounds is exaggerated and often serves VC incentives more than startup interests. While down rounds can hurt employees, the real damage is usually optics-driven: fear of reputational harm pushes founders into worse terms and riskier bets than they should take.
quotes.key_insight: Down rounds are often less financially disastrous than their stigma implies, and the fear around them mainly serves VC incentives rather than startup realities.

quotes.quote: Pricing is like that.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses a bar-pricing parable to argue that pricing models are economically flexible but socially constrained by expectations. He contends that dbt’s usage-based pricing is theoretically sensible and incentive-aligned, yet the real conflict is with entrenched norms from its free/open-source roots, which will shape who it ultimately builds for.
quotes.key_insight: The “right” pricing model is less about economics than expectations, and those expectations determine which customers a company will ultimately serve.

quotes.quote: If LLMs do end up changing how we use computers, this type of approach—one that introduces new ways of accomplishing some task rather than accelerating old ways; the car rather than the faster horse—feels like a glimpse of the future.
quotes.tone: analytical and skeptical with a reflective, lightly humorous edge
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that most LLM analytics chatbots are misaligned with real analytical work because they answer requests without the context needed to produce useful or correct outputs. He proposes a “smol analyst” workflow—iterative, feedback-driven drafting like a junior analyst—which could make LLMs more practical, while noting that data’s need for transparent logic makes this harder than in software.
quotes.key_insight: LLM analytics will be most useful when treated like junior analysts in an iterative feedback loop, but their outputs still require transparency and review because data work cannot tolerate black boxes.

quotes.quote: If we have to ask a bunch of follow-up questions before we dig up some number for people, why are we excited about LLMs that mechanically do exactly that?

quotes.summary: Stancil argues that most LLM analytics chatbots are misaligned with real analytical work because they answer requests without the context needed to produce useful or correct outputs. He proposes a “smol analyst” workflow—iterative, feedback-driven drafting like a junior analyst—which could make LLMs more practical, while noting that data’s need for transparent logic makes this harder than in software.
quotes.key_insight: LLM analytics will be most useful when treated like junior analysts in an iterative feedback loop, but their outputs still require transparency and review because data work cannot tolerate black boxes.

quotes.quote: In data development, people are working off of an infinitely splintering collection of caches.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s core pain isn’t tooling or process but the need to work on layered caches; unlike software, data pipelines rarely run live end-to-end. If instant, on-demand processing were possible, reliability, debugging, and trust would improve dramatically, but until then, copying software engineering practices only helps at the margins.
quotes.key_insight: The data stack’s single point of failure is its reliance on cached, stepwise processing, and only near-instant end-to-end computation would make data work as reliable as software engineering.

quotes.quote: Correlation may not imply causation, but it’s almost always its leading indicator.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the business world has overcorrected against intuition by treating emotions as inadmissible evidence, even though feelings often act as leading indicators of what data will later confirm. He contends that companies should surface emotions alongside analytics to make more honest decisions, avoid self-deception, and unlock bolder moves. Emotions shouldn’t drive choices blindly, but they should inform them as real signals.
quotes.key_insight: Treat emotions as informative signals alongside data, because honest, courageous decisions require both analysis and feeling.

quotes.quote: There’s a lot of signal in our emotions, even if it’s not scientifically robust or “statistically significant.”

quotes.quote: Data quality is a tradeoff.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that clean data is less a solvable engineering problem than a tradeoff that teams often choose, because businesses evolve faster than their data systems can. Tools like SDF help mitigate issues, but they are edge improvements that don’t change the messy, exploratory nature of data work. He suggests shifting from chasing stability to designing for resiliency and easier refactoring.
quotes.key_insight: Clean data isn’t a permanent destination; the realistic goal is building systems that tolerate change and make messy realities easier to debug and refactor.

quotes.quote: Companies’ data infrastructures—including those at many young startups—are often terminally messy, full of brittle scripts, manually updated CSVs, and “pipelines” that involve transferring an Excel file from one computer to another with a USB drive.

quotes.quote: Data consumption tools aren’t infrastructure; they’re productivity tools.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses the ThoughtSpot–Mode merger to explore how acquisitions in BI can fail, arguing that strategic fit is less important than protecting user experience, respecting differing product philosophies, and acknowledging market shifts. He warns that integration traps, mismatched customer needs, suite consolidation, and AI-driven paradigm shifts are the real risks, while the biggest failures often come from well-known, unaddressed weaknesses.
quotes.key_insight: Acquisitions in BI succeed or fail less on headline strategy and more on preserving product experience, respecting customer philosophy, and confronting obvious operational risks amid looming platform shifts.

quotes.quote: Protecting that experience across the two products has to be the first priority of any acquisition like this; enhancing it has to be the second; and neatly sorting through the technical details of how to do it has to be a distant third.

quotes.quote: For bots to be successful query writers—and even harder, for them to be proper analysts that can answer questions about a business—LLMs will likely only be a small part of the solution.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that most SQL chatbots are aiming at the wrong problem: LLMs can draft queries in toy schemas, but real-world analysis requires extensive context, governance, and BI-like infrastructure. He contends that LLMs are better at creative, hypothesis-generating work than at precise, deductive querying, which flips the traditional promise that tech automates the tedious parts so humans can do the fun parts.
quotes.key_insight: LLMs are ill-suited to reliable query generation at scale but excel at creative analytical reasoning, so chatbots risk automating the fun parts while leaving humans with the drudgery.

quotes.quote: Put differently, the LLM isn’t an application, or even close to one.

quotes.quote: A database isn’t just a collection of information; it’s a tightly organized catalog of capital-D Data.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that we conflate “databases” with structured, analyzable data, while the far larger opportunity is an “informationbase” that indexes and retrieves useful facts, documents, and context without heavy modeling. Snowflake’s Document AI hints at a shift from data warehousing toward Google‑like information retrieval, even if the most likely explanation is simply customer demand. The broader point is that search and retrieval for operational questions may matter more to most organizations than analytics-grade data pipelines.
quotes.key_insight: The most valuable next step for data platforms may be indexing and retrieving information, not just storing analyzable data.

quotes.quote: So I said it wouldn’t cost me anything. But I didn't say what it would cost you.
quotes.summary: Stancil reflects on how a decade of startup ambition and workaholism strained his relationship with his parents, costing them time and attention he never gave back. With Mode’s sale, he pauses his usual arguments to offer a heartfelt apology and gratitude, acknowledging their sacrifices and affirming the personal meaning of what was built.
quotes.key_insight: The true cost of startup success is often paid in neglected relationships, making gratitude and repair as important as the outcome.

quotes.quote: What’s notable about this story is that it’s not declaring the microwave dead; to the contrary, the microwave is everywhere. It’s obituary for the microwave’s potential.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses microwaves as a metaphor for data: ubiquitous, useful, but potentially capped in transformative value. He argues data tooling may have hit a plateau as a utilitarian appliance, and that only a shift to fast, natural‑language access (via LLMs) could unlock a true second act by empowering many more people to ask and answer questions.
quotes.key_insight: Data may be stuck as a commodity appliance unless LLM‑driven, natural‑language BI enables mass participation in analysis.

quotes.quote: There’s a different future that’s also out there: One in which data is everywhere, but the revolution is nowhere.

quotes.quote: The verdict: Nice guys finish last.
quotes.summary: Stancil tests whether polite prompts improve ChatGPT performance and finds the opposite: neutral phrasing yields the best accuracy, while nice or mean tones slightly degrade results and increase verbosity. Despite the data, he frames the choice to be polite as a Pascal’s Wager: a tiny performance cost might be worth it if kindness matters in a future where AI could punish us.
quotes.key_insight: Neutral prompts slightly outperform polite or harsh ones, but he jokes that politeness may still be rational if you treat it as a low-cost hedge against a sci‑fi future.

quotes.quote: When asked to do something in a neutral tone, ChatGPT correctly completed the task 73.4 percent of the time.

quotes.quote: No, everyone else uses Method 2: “Do I believe this number, given what I believed yesterday?”
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data teams over-invest in validating inputs and lineage, yet trust in metrics actually comes from output consistency over time. Because users judge credibility by whether numbers stay stable across reports, he proposes tools and practices that surface changes and drift in outputs rather than just monitoring pipelines and tests.
quotes.key_insight: Trust in metrics comes less from perfect pipelines and more from visibly consistent outputs over time, so data tooling should highlight change and drift in results.

quotes.quote: In other words, trust isn’t built by confirming our sources; it’s built via mathematical induction.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data teams over-invest in validating inputs and lineage, yet trust in metrics actually comes from output consistency over time. Because users judge credibility by whether numbers stay stable across reports, he proposes tools and practices that surface changes and drift in outputs rather than just monitoring pipelines and tests.
quotes.key_insight: Trust in metrics comes less from perfect pipelines and more from visibly consistent outputs over time, so data tooling should highlight change and drift in results.

quotes.quote: A daring talk that flops and a cautious talk that safely sticks to all the comfortable best practices are equally ignored.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that most talks are forgettable and audiences expect to be bored, which creates a low-risk environment for bold choices. Because cautious and daring talks are equally ignored, the best strategy is to gamble on entertainment, pace, and narrative cohesion. The goal is to keep attention and create a memorable experience rather than deliver didactic takeaways.
quotes.key_insight: Since audiences already expect talks to be boring, speakers should take creative risks that prioritize entertainment and momentum over safe, instructional delivery.

quotes.quote: But this plan has always had one big problem: There is no potato.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s promise of modular, user-driven tools has been undermined by the absence of a true platform—there’s “no potato” to hold the pieces together. Microsoft Fabric responds by collapsing the stack into a single integrated product, which could appeal to enterprises and shift the market from “legacy vs. modern” to “all‑in‑one vs. best‑of‑breed.” He concludes that the real opportunity is a neutral platform layer that enables genuine modularity, ideally from a cloud provider building infrastructure rather than a monolithic suite.
quotes.key_insight: Without a shared platform layer, the modern data stack collapses into bloated, overlapping products—and Fabric’s integrated bet forces the market to choose between seamless consolidation and true best‑of‑breed modularity.

quotes.quote: In other words, price changes mostly affect how much of something we consume.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the most disruptive impact of generative AI is not that content becomes free, but that it becomes immediate, which changes how we seek and use information. That shift will erode search-driven, click-based web behavior and favor direct answers, known destinations, and on-demand interfaces, reshaping products like data catalogs and the broader web.
quotes.key_insight: The internet will change most because AI makes content instantaneous, collapsing the need for search-based discovery and static documentation in favor of direct, on-demand answers.

quotes.quote: Speed changes how we consume it-and the effects of that can be much more profound.

quotes.quote: The bitter lesson is based on the historical observations that 1) AI researchers have often tried to build knowledge into their agents, 2) this always helps in the short term, and is personally satisfying to the researcher, but 3) in the long run it plateaus and even inhibits further progress, and 4) breakthrough progress eventually arrives by an opposing approach based on scaling computation by search and learning.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses the Kasparov–Deep Blue story and Sutton’s “bitter lesson” to argue that AI progress comes less from encoding human domain knowledge and more from scaling computation and search. He then applies that lens to data teams, warning that human-built semantic models may constrain impact and suggesting that more compute-centric approaches could uncover strategies we can’t easily see.
quotes.key_insight: Data teams may be stalling by over-relying on human-built abstractions, and greater gains may come from compute-heavy approaches that search beyond our semantic assumptions.

quotes.quote: These programs are unnatural, but deadly superior.

quotes.quote: Our business models and semantic layers are efforts to describe businesses as we see them; they are a “favored, human-centric approach,” and are our methods—as opposed to the methods—for reasoning about difficult and tangled problems.

quotes.quote: Instead, perhaps we should be trying to remove ourselves and our semantics as much as possible, and let AI agents work on problems on their own terms.

quotes.quote: Presenting data in this way—in a tidy, consumable, and constrained format that’s accessible to everyone—is typically what BI tools are for.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that today’s “universal semantic layers” focus on metric extraction and accessibility via APIs, but that makes them effectively BI tools without charts rather than truly universal layers. Because data consumption preferences are diverse and tools require different computational models, a semantic layer that only serves metric tables can’t support the broader ecosystem. A real universal layer would combine shared entities with flexible computation like SQL, and he’s skeptical current approaches can do that yet.
quotes.key_insight: Metric-focused semantic layers solve BI-style reporting but fall short of being truly universal because they can’t support the diverse computational models that real data tooling demands.

quotes.quote: In other words, today’s independent semantic layers aren’t universally accessible encodings of business logic that can be used by any downstream analytical application; they’re BI tools, without charts.

quotes.quote: In other words, the two-step startup is a myth.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the “two-step startup” is a myth: companies rarely evolve into a fundamentally different second act, and their founding product definition tends to persist from seed to IPO. Because partial pivots mostly fail, founders and investors should be brutally honest about whether the original idea can scale or must be fully rebuilt. Data dashboards won’t surface this truth; the real signal is felt in conviction and customer pull.
quotes.key_insight: If the seed-stage product description can’t plausibly be the IPO description, a startup needs a full pivot—because partial pivots only prolong failure.

quotes.quote: Put differently, for a business to grow into something big, its initial product has to grow into something big.

quotes.quote: A crash course, no matter how intense and thorough, would be a poor substitute for just reading the news for twenty years.
quotes.tone: reflective, critical, and analytical with humor
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analysts can resemble “nicer, kinder” Jared Kushners: parachuting into others’ problems with shallow context and overconfident analysis that adds little value. He suggests shifting data teams from decision-support consultants to newsroom-style publishers who build company-wide, ambient understanding through continual narrative updates. A better-informed organization, not better point decisions, is the real competitive edge.
quotes.key_insight: Data teams should prioritize publishing continuous, narrative context that keeps everyone informed, rather than parachuting into discrete decisions with shallow analysis.

quotes.quote: Though dbt Labs' success is impressive, this bubbling appreciation from its users is what makes it truly unique.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that dbt Labs’ success rests as much on its community and human-centered approach as its product, but the market tailwinds that protected it are fading. He lays out three futures—stay the course, go on the offensive into adjacent markets, or sell—while noting that the rise of LLM-driven data stacks could reshape what “dbt” needs to be.
quotes.key_insight: dbt’s edge has been its human-centered community-driven model, but shifting market dynamics and AI-driven architectures may force it to either expand aggressively or find a strategic exit.

quotes.quote: The punchline is the entire modern data stack.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack, once the corrective to Hadoop-era pain, has grown into an overgrown ecosystem of tools, costs, and complexity. He suggests we are entering a liminal period where AI/LLMs will trigger a new discontinuity, making the modern data stack’s philosophy ill-suited for the next era. The core claim is that data teams and vendors must adapt to AI-native infrastructures or risk becoming yesterday’s stack.
quotes.key_insight: The modern data stack’s tool-heavy philosophy is peaking and will likely be eclipsed by AI-driven “intelligent infrastructure” that reshapes how data systems and teams are built.

quotes.quote: The introduction of Dolly suggests that LLMs and other generative AI models are also “skilled,” and could be divided along the same crude lines that economists use to divide the labor force.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that as LLMs proliferate, they will sort into “low-skill” and “high-skill” tiers much like human labor markets, with cost and capability gaps driving differentiation. This could create new forms of inequality and competitive advantage, as premium models compound small advantages over time, even if baseline tools become cheap and widespread.
quotes.key_insight: LLMs are likely to form stratified labor markets where marginal capability differences create compounding economic and social advantages.

quotes.quote: Because public AI providers like OpenAI aren’t destined to become the next iPhone, but the next—and maybe, much bigger—AWS.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that OpenAI’s real destiny isn’t to become a consumer app platform like the iPhone, but an AI infrastructure provider akin to AWS. Generative models will become a ubiquitous, mostly invisible backbone powering countless products, and the biggest prize is dominance as a foundational utility rather than direct user attention.
quotes.key_insight: OpenAI’s highest-leverage path is to become the generative infrastructure layer of the internet, not a consumer-facing app platform, because control of the backend yields scale, lock-in, and enormous power.

quotes.quote: The acute danger of this crisis, then, wasn’t that some people would be fired, though that would indeed be bad for those people; it was who would be fired.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the SVB bailout was justified either to prevent systemic panic or to protect startups deemed uniquely vital, but that second rationale implicitly demands special treatment that Silicon Valley doesn’t want to admit. He suggests SVB may be a “Lehman moment” for tech, exposing a subprime-like financial scaffolding that could trigger regulatory reckoning and shows how risky innovation finance has become.
quotes.key_insight: The SVB episode reveals a tension between Silicon Valley’s claim to exceptional importance and its resistance to the regulation that such exceptional treatment would logically require.

quotes.quote: Their investment is, as the cliche goes, a $12 billion solution in search of a problem, inspired, it seems, by fear and FOMO.
quotes.summary: Stancil contrasts JPMorgan’s massive data investment with a hypothetical NBA team’s analytics push to argue that data initiatives work when they’re aimed at clear, concrete problems, and fail when they’re driven by hype and vague promises. He’s skeptical of open-ended “digital transformations,” but sees value in the messy experimentation of the data boom, even if much of it proves wasteful.
quotes.key_insight: Data investments deliver value only when tied to clear hypotheses and problems, but even wasteful experiments can yield lasting innovations.

quotes.quote: The alarming truth, I’m realizing, is that the three layers underneath our disorganized outer boundary aren’t actually so stable after all.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the concept of a “data warehouse” has become fuzzy and skeuomorphic: we recognize examples by vibe, not by a stable definition. As architectures shift toward storage-less, serverless, and file-based compute, he suggests warehouses are already breaking apart and may evolve into something like dbt running directly on object storage.
quotes.key_insight: The warehouse has become a legacy label for a shifting set of capabilities, and the next “warehouse” may look more like dbt-driven compute on object storage than a monolithic database.

quotes.quote: I can’t bend my feelings around facts; I can’t forge them between a hammer of reason and the anvil of reality.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses his anxiety about flying to argue that emotions often overpower facts, making rational self-talk an unreliable defense against fear. He warns that modern AI, especially chatbots, is uniquely adept at shaping feelings, so the larger risk is not misinformation but engineered emotional manipulation at scale.
quotes.key_insight: AI’s greatest near-term power is its ability to manipulate human emotion, and our weakness against that manipulation makes collective harm more likely than mere confusion about facts.

quotes.quote: Eventually, the customers fully capture the company, trapping it not in a single identity, but in many.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that startups can fall into “customer capture,” where divergent early customers pull a company into incompatible directions, eroding product coherence and market identity. This is especially acute in the fragmented data tooling landscape, where many adjacent expansion paths look like optionality but become unsustainable variability. The antidote is a disciplined, caring core identity and the willingness to say no to customers and investors until the company’s center of gravity is strong enough to expand.
quotes.key_insight: Customer-driven iteration can quietly splinter a startup’s identity, so sustained success requires disciplined focus on a coherent core before expanding.

quotes.quote: But, crass as it may sound, truth is just a means to an end—to make a company successful.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data teams often overvalue objective truth-telling, when their most useful role in business may be to align people around a chosen strategy. Because execution and belief shape outcomes, selective, narrative-driven use of data can be more valuable than neutral analysis, even if it feels uncomfortable.
quotes.key_insight: In business, data’s highest value often lies not in objective truth-finding but in building conviction and alignment that make strategies succeed.

quotes.quote: Insights are grandiose enough to sound valuable, and amorphous enough to avoid actually saying what that value is.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry has inflated the word “insight” into a vague marketing promise, even though most users and businesses mainly want reliable, basic reporting about what’s happening. He contends that data teams chase flashy analysis because it is socially and commercially rewarded, while the harder, more valuable work of accurate reporting is undervalued.
quotes.key_insight: The industry’s obsession with “insight” obscures the real value of data work, which is delivering clear, accurate reporting about what’s happening.

quotes.quote: The distance between this and companies’ actual data ecosystems is staggering.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that while LLM demos look impressive, they fail in real-world data environments because messy schemas, ambiguous definitions, and complex queries overwhelm current models. The real revolution will come not from smarter models alone but from rebuilding data structures to be AI-friendly—standardized, machine-readable schemas that shift analytics work from bespoke design to factory-like production.
quotes.key_insight: AI won’t transform analytics by replacing analysts outright; it will force a redesign of data schemas themselves so machines—not people—can reliably interpret and query them.

quotes.summary: Stancil argues that while LLM demos look impressive, they fail in real-world data environments because messy schemas, ambiguous definitions, and complex queries overwhelm current models. The real revolution will come not from smarter models alone but from rebuilding data structures to be AI-friendly—standardized, machine-readable schemas that shift analytics work from bespoke design to factory-like production.
quotes.key_insight: AI won’t transform analytics by replacing analysts outright; it will force a redesign of data schemas themselves so machines—not people—can reliably interpret and query them.

quotes.quote: Products that have product-market fit are bought, not sold.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that many data teams may lack true product-market fit: they are often sold rather than eagerly bought, and persistent struggles to gain influence suggest a deeper mismatch with stakeholder demand. He contends that the field may be extrapolating from a few early “customers” and should rethink what services are actually wanted, even if that means reinventing the role.
quotes.key_insight: The persistent need for data teams to sell their value suggests a product-market mismatch, and the discipline may need to redefine what it offers based on real customer demand.

quotes.quote: They found a hole in the market that was overwhelmingly complementary with the rest of the industry, and quickly built a popular product that discouraged head-on competition.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the data tooling boom favored neutral, modular ecosystems, but the market’s fragmentation and tighter budgets are pushing vendors into direct competition. Instead of simple consolidation into big-platform bundles, he proposes a “conglomerate” model where adjacent data products live under one umbrella, staying independent yet more seamlessly integrated. This could preserve product character while delivering the cohesion customers increasingly demand.
quotes.key_insight: The data stack’s future may be best served not by pure modularity or full consolidation, but by conglomerates that align adjacent tools under one roof to deliver cohesion without forcing total lock-in.

quotes.quote: Without that, the data stack, assembled from a dozen tools produced by the same number of startups, will remain frustratingly disjointed and incongruous.

quotes.quote: In other words, it’s not our ability to do fancy math that makes us effective, but our ability to apply basic math.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that math is professionally overrated in data work: most roles rely on basic arithmetic and judgment, not advanced theory. The real value of “mathiness” is cultural signaling—using quantitative language and artifacts to claim authority and shut down qualitative debate—so we should prize communication and domain understanding more than we do.
quotes.key_insight: Math’s prestige in data work functions more as a signaling and power tool than as a reflection of the actual skills most analysts need.

quotes.quote: In total, I’d estimate that less than half of the nearly 600 predictions issued by various experts over the last ten years could be reasonably scored as correct.
quotes.summary: Stancil reviews a decade of data-industry predictions and finds that fewer than half were mostly correct, with many being vague, trend‑extrapolation filler. He argues that forecasts repeatedly promise imminent breakthroughs (AI, predictive analytics, streaming, data marketplaces) that arrive later or not at all, while genuinely important developments often go unpredicted. The upshot: expect 2023 to miss in familiar ways, with surprises filling the gaps.
quotes.key_insight: A decade of data forecasts shows a pattern of over‑eager, vague predictions that miss timing, while the biggest shifts often emerge unexpectedly.

quotes.quote: The problem with penalty kicks isn't their randomness; it's that they have next to nothing to do with the sport that everyone just played for 120 minutes.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that penalty shootouts are a different skill than the 120 minutes of soccer that precede them, creating a strategic arbitrage opportunity. Because shootouts are common and roster spots are underutilized, teams could meaningfully raise their World Cup odds by carrying dedicated penalty kick specialists. The idea is a low-cost, “Moneyball” style edge that conventional roster construction ignores.
quotes.key_insight: Because shootouts are common and hinge on a distinct, trainable skill, dedicating roster spots to penalty-kick specialists is a small, quantifiable change that can materially improve a team’s World Cup chances.

quotes.quote: Slack is a kanban board with two stages: Gone forever, and do it now.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that despite sweeping technological advances, productivity hasn’t improved because the tools we use shape behavior more than any process or best-practice talk. Slack exemplifies this by incentivizing frantic, low-quality communication, so the real path to better work is to build products that enforce better processes, especially for data teams. In data, he claims only purpose-built tools will shift norms around requests, alerting, and decision-making.
quotes.key_insight: Lasting improvements in how data teams work will come from purpose-built products that reshape behavior, not from exhortations about process.

quotes.quote: But if we score Slack against communication tools’ more meaningful measures—does it help us make good decisions, and keep everyone informed of and aligned around those decisions?—Slack is a spectacular step backwards.

quotes.quote: We’re not smart enough for AI.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analysis relies on the difficulty of constructing convincing arguments, which gives it a kind of legitimacy even when people are biased and imperfect reasoners. Large language models erase that friction by generating plausible narratives on demand, making it harder to distinguish truth from motivated reasoning and undermining decision‑making.
quotes.key_insight: LLMs make it trivially easy to generate convincing but ungrounded arguments, eroding the friction that once helped analysis stay tethered to reality.

quotes.quote: There is, however, one useful protection against us constantly being fooled by questionable reasoning: It's a lot harder to come up with decent arguments for something that's wrong than something that's right.

quotes.quote: A spreadsheet nobody needs
quotes.summary: Stancil riffs on watching Lou Gehrig’s retirement speech and realizing he is the same age, which sends him into compiling a sprawling, unnecessary spreadsheet of famous people’s ages at notable moments. The post uses that absurd catalog to poke at age-based comparison, mortality anxiety, and the futility of measuring life against milestones.
quotes.key_insight: Age comparisons across famous milestones are both irresistible and ultimately meaningless, revealing more about personal anxiety than about achievement.

quotes.quote: The tools we have today—built and supported by thousands of people across dozens of companies—represent a profound leap forward from what we had then.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that despite the rough edges and hype, the modern data stack represents a huge leap forward from the brittle, expensive, and manual systems of the early 2010s. Yet he highlights a paradox: data teams delivered substantial business impact even with inferior tooling, so the key question is why today’s far better tech hasn’t produced proportional value gains.
quotes.key_insight: Modern data tooling is dramatically better, but the industry still hasn’t closed the gap between technological progress and real business impact.

quotes.quote: The doors are coming off, and the wheels are rattling loose.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the internet has entered a new, hotter phase of chaos where platforms collapse fast, frauds flourish, and attention cycles overwhelm any stable order. He portrays a culture trapped in a runaway machine, where even serious news is instantly drowned by the next manic catastrophe. The only viable stance, he suggests, is to accept the turmoil and write for the void rather than for a stable audience.
quotes.key_insight: The contemporary internet is less a stable platform than a runaway spectacle, so the only honest response is to acknowledge and write into its chaos.

quotes.quote: Twitter is being called from inside the house; it is being murdered; organs are failing; it’s losing blood; it could practically die overnight, at the height of its cultural power (and popularly, evidently).

quotes.key_insight: The contemporary internet is less a stable platform than a runaway spectacle, so the only honest response is to acknowledge and write into its chaos.

quotes.quote: Do not write for a quaint audience on the internet; write for the howling void.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the internet has entered a new, hotter phase of chaos where platforms collapse fast, frauds flourish, and attention cycles overwhelm any stable order. He portrays a culture trapped in a runaway machine, where even serious news is instantly drowned by the next manic catastrophe. The only viable stance, he suggests, is to accept the turmoil and write for the void rather than for a stable audience.
quotes.key_insight: The contemporary internet is less a stable platform than a runaway spectacle, so the only honest response is to acknowledge and write into its chaos.

quotes.quote: Tools are out; people and processes are in.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s shift from tools to people and process misses a deeper constraint: most companies don’t have data with the leverage of Silicon Valley giants. The modern data stack looks powerful, but for many businesses the data is more like peat than oil—limited value means copying big-tech practices often yields disappointing returns.
quotes.key_insight: Most companies overestimate the value of their data, so emulating big-tech data practices and tooling often leads to waste rather than outsized gains.

quotes.quote: In other words, rather than being exemplars of good data practices because they were so good, it’s possible that they’re exemplars because they didn’t have to be that good.

quotes.quote: The data of a mid-sized B2B SaaS product simply doesn’t have the potential energy of Google’s search histories, or of an Amazon’s browsing logs.

quotes.quote: Peat suppliers can’t copy the refinement methods and cost structures of oil producers and still expect to come out ahead; small SaaS businesses can’t operate as though their data has anywhere near the same value as Uber’s or Airbnb’s.

quotes.quote: They were never told that they were wrong, only that they were interesting.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that elite institutions and Silicon Valley share a performative culture that rewards confident, untested ideas rather than rigorous scrutiny, producing a class of leaders who are affirmed rather than challenged. He sees the VC pitch dynamic and Twitter’s Musk-era chaos as parallel examples of the “very smart boy” myth and its consequences. Twitter becomes the live-fire test of whether tech’s celebrated first‑principles bravado is wisdom or just noise.
quotes.key_insight: The tech elite’s confidence and folklore about visionary genius are being stress‑tested in public, and Twitter may expose how shallow those claims really are.

quotes.quote: Most of these suggestions are naked copies of industry fads or the VC's latest successes.

quotes.quote: Nobody is a better judge of a data asset’s worth than its consumer.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data teams inside startups operate like collectivist service orgs, which leads to fuzzy prioritization and hidden politics. He proposes an internal market with credits and auctions to price data work, claiming it could allocate effort more efficiently and transparently while still letting teams steer strategy. He ends cautiously, noting the model could work with safeguards but needs regulation to avoid a cutthroat culture.
quotes.key_insight: A credit-and-auction market for data work could replace political prioritization with transparent price signals, but it needs guardrails to avoid perverse incentives.

quotes.quote: By separating the work from the reward, we can be more honest with ourselves about why we do the things we do.
quotes.tone: reflective and analytical, with a note of wistful criticism
quotes.summary: Stancil contrasts ambition-driven careerism with work chosen for its intrinsic value, arguing that communities thrive when people show up for the work rather than the payoff. He uses dbt Labs’ Coalesce as a rare, genuine data community that feels like college, while acknowledging that scale and business pressures will eventually erode that culture. The piece ends with a call to appreciate the community now, even knowing it won’t last forever.
quotes.key_insight: Even knowing a community’s golden era is temporary, its value comes from people showing up for the work and relationships themselves, not just the eventual rewards.

quotes.quote: When we become fixated on a destination, the journey is an interchangeable means to an end, a disposable rocket booster that’s only value is to put us into space.

quotes.quote: There are three fallacies that make internal tools look more promising than they actually are.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that internal tools often look like startup gold because they solve a real problem inside a company, but that success is a mirage driven by a captive customer, edge-case needs, and effortless internal distribution. Once outside the company, those tools face messy, varied workflows, politics, and integration realities that make them poor fits for broader markets. He suggests investors should be more skeptical and look to consultancies for better-validated product ideas, noting his own experience at Mode as a cautionary example.
quotes.key_insight: Internal success is a misleading signal because the very factors that make a tool thrive inside one company—captive users, bespoke needs, and frictionless distribution—rarely translate to a viable external market.

quotes.quote: It’s easy to find product market-fit when your market is your coworker sitting right next to you, telling you exactly why the other products they’ve tried don’t work.

quotes.quote: Given such a golden run, the only data industry story that would be more captivating than one about dbt Labs’ success would be one about its failure.
quotes.summary: Stancil imagines a plausible failure case for dbt Labs, arguing that the company’s success could be undermined by a mismatch between its SQL-first core and the added complexity of semantic layers, pricing shifts, and community fatigue. He contends that these pressures would weaken dbt’s moat and invite both direct and indirect competitors. He ultimately concludes the failure narrative is unlikely because dbt’s product value is real and enduring.
quotes.key_insight: dbt’s biggest risk isn’t demand but the tension between its SQL-first simplicity and the complexity of expanding into semantics, pricing, and new markets, which could erode its community moat.

quotes.quote: When dbt was first released, its promise was simple and elegant: It’s SQL, all the way down.

quotes.quote: Of all of the iPhone’s accomplishments, one of the most remarkable is that it put the same exact piece of technology into the pockets of people in every strata of society.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the iPhone is remarkable for delivering a fundamentally egalitarian product experience across social strata, unlike Twitter, where each user inhabits a materially different version shaped by identity and prominence. Because these experiences are unmeasurable and often inaccessible, leaders and data practitioners must approach product decisions with humility and seek lived experience beyond dashboards.
quotes.key_insight: Some products demand humility because their most important user experiences are unequal, unquantifiable, and only knowable through empathy rather than data alone.

quotes.quote: Data contracts make exactly that trade. They replace a brittle technical system with a negotiating table.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data contracts address a real problem—brittle, implicit expectations between data producers and consumers—but negotiated agreements between teams are impractical and misdirected. Instead of people-heavy contracts, he favors technical safeguards built by data teams (e.g., schema/staging tests) that validate data before it hits production without forcing providers into ongoing negotiations.
quotes.key_insight: The best “data contract” isn’t a negotiated promise between teams but a technical pre-production testing layer that data teams control to prevent bad data from propagating.

quotes.quote: If those structures need to change to make a product better or to smooth over a kink in the sales cycle, they shouldn’t have to consult an analytics engineer first.

quotes.quote: Fivetran is, if nothing else, a product of its time.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Fivetran’s dominance is not inevitable despite riding two waves: SaaS proliferation and centralized data warehouses. He lays out three ways Fivetran could fail—if the need to move data fades, if SaaS vendors and warehouses change how data is moved, or if a better/cheaper tool displaces it—while ultimately betting on inertia but acknowledging how quickly the “impractical” can become the new normal.
quotes.key_insight: Fivetran’s position is strong but structurally vulnerable if SaaS vendors and warehouses shift data movement upstream, eroding the economics that make ELT a standalone business.

quotes.quote: We’d all be better off—well, all of us except ELT vendors—if more apps did what Segment does, and wrote data straight to the warehouse themselves.

quotes.quote: If data is a product, we should have a better definition of production.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues the “data as a product” idea is incomplete without a clear definition of what counts as production. Because data work often blurs one-off analysis and durable assets, teams end up maintaining a sprawling, accidental production surface. He proposes explicitly declaring production assets and letting everything else be temporary, with real support and deprecation for the chosen few.
quotes.key_insight: Data teams need an explicit, narrow definition of production so they can support what matters and let the rest expire instead of growing an unmanageable, accidental production surface.

quotes.quote: Production is a fuzzy concept, with a wide and blurry boundary between it and development.

quotes.quote: Sure things, in other words, aren’t always dominant.
quotes.tone: analytical and critical with a wry, satirical edge
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Snowflake’s apparent inevitability is less certain than the industry consensus suggests, and runs a “pre‑mortem” on how a dominant data warehouse could lose its edge. He outlines multiple plausible failure paths—cost dynamics, architectural fit, market overreach, and margin pressure—while still acknowledging Snowflake’s current strength.
quotes.key_insight: Even if Snowflake looks like an inevitable winner today, multiple structural and strategic pressures could still erode its dominance over time.

quotes.quote: The future is precious, not the past.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that habits and identities often calcify from chance events, and we mistake their persistence for virtue. He suggests we are less bound by the past than we believe, and should prioritize future possibilities over inherited routines or traditions. The right question is whether a habit still serves who we want to become, not how long we have maintained it.
quotes.key_insight: Endurance and tradition aren’t inherently virtuous; we should keep only the habits and identities that actively serve the future we want.

quotes.quote: For as much as we talk about how to avoid getting things wrong—which is an implicit acknowledgement that we get things wrong a lot—we spend hardly any time talking about what happens when we do.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that while analytics teams obsess over preventing errors, they barely grapple with what to do when mistakes inevitably slip into production. Because analytical errors shape decisions and persist, he questions whether engineering-style blameless retros are sufficient and suggests borrowing from journalism’s accountability. He also proposes rounding metrics to signal uncertainty and reduce the blast radius of inevitable corrections.
quotes.key_insight: Because analytical errors are inevitable and have lasting consequences, data teams should treat metrics as uncertain estimates and rethink correction practices, potentially emphasizing accountability and rounded communication over engineering-style blamelessness.

quotes.quote: Despite its sheen of scientific rigor, data is a confidence game; the only currency we have is trust.

quotes.quote: We want the updated datasets or fresh dashboards at the end of our lineage graph, but define our schedules around what’s at the beginning of it.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data orchestration is backwards: we schedule pipelines from upstream triggers instead of from the freshness guarantees we actually care about at the end of the lineage. He proposes a reverse-ETL-style model where each table declares latency requirements and the system derives the DAG and run timing to meet those guarantees, improving maintainability, cost control, and alert relevance.
quotes.key_insight: Define data freshness guarantees first and let orchestration derive the DAG and schedules, rather than chaining tasks from upstream triggers.

quotes.quote: As analysts, we committed parameterized SQL statements and YAML configuration files to Integritie, which would use the queries, and the dependencies implied in them, to regularly create and update tables in our warehouse.

quotes.quote: I think we can solve this though—analysts should create portfolios.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analysts are screened on poor signals like resumes and brand names because their work is hard to showcase, which shuts out strong talent. He proposes replacing boot-camp style credentialing and take-home tests with concise analytical portfolios that emphasize thinking, storytelling, and real project context. A short, structured slide deck could surface skill and potential more fairly while improving interviews.
quotes.key_insight: Analytical portfolios would surface real capability and storytelling skill more fairly than resumes or tests, reshaping hiring toward how analysts actually work.

quotes.quote: But to get there, we also have to agree on who should provide that pane of glass—and right now, there’s nothing close to a consensus on that question.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack lacks a true “single pane of glass,” and that multiple tool categories are converging to claim that central control role. This competitive rush, driven by venture funding expectations, makes fragmentation worse until the market forces a single winner.
quotes.key_insight: The data stack’s chaos is less a tooling problem than a market structure problem, and VC-driven competition will force a single “control tower” winner even as it intensifies fragmentation in the meantime.

quotes.quote: The biggest looming battle, however, will be over a different territory: The brain—or operating system—of the data stack.

quotes.quote: Great data teams likely make somewhat better guesses, but at the end of the day, we’re all still gambling.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that while data helps, it is not a decisive competitive advantage against stronger talent, intuition, or speed—especially in early-stage companies. Data’s real value is a small, compounding edge across many decisions, not dramatic breakthroughs on a few big bets.
quotes.key_insight: Data-driven companies can win, but only because steady, modest advantages compound over many decisions rather than because data guarantees better outcomes on big strategic bets.

quotes.quote: Nearly every asset that a data team creates—a report, a dashboard, a data model, a predictive forecast, you name it—is riddled with hardcoded bits of business logic.
quotes.summary: Benn argues that data teams hardcode business logic all over their stacks, leading to inconsistent definitions and opaque concepts for anyone outside the data team. He proposes centralizing these logical variables (and eventually logic itself) in a shared configuration file so definitions stay consistent, discoverable, and editable. This could shift workflows toward more composable, collaborative analytics engineering, much like dbt did for transformations.
quotes.key_insight: Centralizing business logic in a shared config file could fix definition drift and make analytics workflows more consistent, discoverable, and collaborative.

quotes.quote: Because it’s so simple to encode logic like this in queries…it’s impossible to keep the values of these variables consistent.

quotes.quote: More than finding a human connection, I’m protecting a brand.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that as more data people work at data vendors, community interactions are increasingly shaped by corporate affiliation, turning peers into prospects and conversations into subtle marketing. The result is a loss of genuine, human connection in the data community, which he thinks can be restored only by centering shared personal experiences over tools and company agendas.
quotes.key_insight: The data community can only stay healthy if people deliberately separate their identities from their employers and prioritize human connection over vendor promotion.

quotes.quote: The problem isn’t that vendors are present in our space; it’s that vendors are present in us.

quotes.quote: So naturally, ten years later, the new sexiest job of the twenty-first century, the job that analysts want to graduate into, is…the analytics engineer, whose primary responsibility is cleaning and modeling messy data.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the rise of analytics engineering isn’t just hype or better tooling; it reflects analysts’ deeper preference for structured, engineering-flavored work over the messy social reality of analysis. The role offers clear boundaries and tangible outputs, making it an attractive escape from organizational politics and ambiguity. This shift suggests the industry may need to rethink what it means to be an analyst and be more honest about what actually satisfies people in data roles.
quotes.key_insight: Analytics engineering’s popularity says less about the job itself and more about analysts’ desire for structured, engineering-like work over the messy, political reality of analysis.

quotes.quote: The problem, in other words, was never the task; it was how we did it.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the rise of analytics engineering isn’t just hype or better tooling; it reflects analysts’ deeper preference for structured, engineering-flavored work over the messy social reality of analysis. The role offers clear boundaries and tangible outputs, making it an attractive escape from organizational politics and ambiguity. This shift suggests the industry may need to rethink what it means to be an analyst and be more honest about what actually satisfies people in data roles.

quotes.quote: Today, all of the sudden,isn't the dayto tell those jokes.
quotes.summary: Stancil pauses his usual light commentary to mark the Supreme Court’s decision as a grave moral and democratic failure. He argues that six unelected justices are imposing their will, stripping rights, and signaling further harm, making the day too serious for his normal industry jokes.
quotes.key_insight: The author sets aside his usual industry commentary to condemn the Supreme Court’s decision as a disastrous assertion of unaccountable power.

quotes.quote: Data apps are actually much more than blinged-out dashboards that sit on top of a warehouse.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Snowflake’s new application framework signals a shift from “data apps” as dashboards on top of the warehouse to apps that run inside the warehouse itself. If Snowflake executes this vision, it becomes more like an iPhone platform than an app store, enabling deeper functionality, easier distribution, and a new wave of data products. He concludes that this could reignite the data tooling ecosystem rather than mark its decline.
quotes.key_insight: Snowflake’s app framework points toward a future where apps run inside the data warehouse, potentially turning Snowflake into a platform layer that reshapes data infrastructure and product ecosystems.

quotes.quote: In the data industry, Google is this kid.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Google has amassed an impressive but incoherent set of data products, like a kid with powerful Pokemon cards but no cohesive deck. The opportunity is to define a clear buyer (the "purple people" in data orgs) and sell an integrated, easy-to-buy stack rather than overpowered but disconnected tools, a miss driven by tech-first culture and compute-driven sales.
quotes.key_insight: Google’s data stack has the pieces to dominate, but without a coherent buyer focus and integrated experience, its overpowered tools fail to translate into market leadership.

quotes.quote: As far as data technologies go, it’s an embarrassment of riches. But like that of the kid with the Pokémon deck of death, Google’s strategy is much harder to discern.

quotes.quote: Augmented analytics tools, which market themselves as needle detectors for our giant haystacks of data, find mostly useless correlations, some spurious ones, and the occasional meaningful one.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that current AI/automation tools in analytics mostly disappoint because they’re built to mimic analysts’ existing tasks, which often require human judgment and context. He suggests a more promising future is AI as a creative partner—posing novel questions, reframing problems, or generating alternative structures—changing what analytics work even looks like.
quotes.key_insight: AI is unlikely to replace analysts by doing their current work, but it may transform analytics by acting as a creative partner that changes the questions and problems themselves.

quotes.quote: This is why analysts (should) get paid. They have to account for unaccountable factors.

quotes.quote: If we don’t talk about pay—because it’s complicated, because it’s taboo, or because we assume it’ll just sort itself out—pay bands and industry habits will settle along the same biased lines that they always have.
quotes.summary: The post argues that confusing data job titles create unstable pay bands and inequitable compensation across the industry. Stancil contends that analysts’ work—translating messy business problems into persuasive, high‑impact decisions—is harder to develop and more valuable than analytics engineering, yet Silicon Valley’s bias toward technical skills inflates engineer pay. He calls for intentionally paying analysts more to counter this bias and to elevate “soft” skills often gendered and undervalued.
quotes.key_insight: The industry’s default bias toward technical work distorts pay, and correcting it means intentionally valuing analysts’ interpersonal, problem-framing, and influence skills more highly.

quotes.quote: These sets of responsibilities require different skills, some of which are much harder to acquire and hone than others. We should pay people accordingly—and, therefore, analysts should make more than analytics engineers.

quotes.quote: In this context, fundraising rounds and startup valuations are more than financing mechanics; they’re yardsticks for our careers, and an unspoken sorting algorithm for social interactions.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley (like DC) has built a status hierarchy around external scoreboards—valuations, funding rounds, and stock prices—that distort how people measure their work and worth. The market crash reveals how fickle those metrics are, and he suggests using the downturn to detach from hype and refocus ambition on controllable, intrinsically meaningful outcomes.
quotes.key_insight: Market crashes expose how arbitrary Silicon Valley’s status metrics are, offering a chance to shift ambition from hype and valuation to meaningful, controllable work.

quotes.quote: To propose an analytically heretical axiom about big choices like these, making the decision is more important than the decision you make.
quotes.summary: Benn argues that for startups and big strategic choices, committing fully to a path matters more than picking the “perfect” option; optionality and constant iteration often dilute focus and stall progress. He extends this to data work, claiming analytics can undermine commitment by highlighting uncertainty, so data teams should prioritize helping organizations stick to decisions to improve outcomes.
quotes.key_insight: Commitment to a chosen path drives results more than continued analysis or optionality, and data teams should support that commitment rather than perpetuate doubt.

quotes.quote: Microsoft is a dominant player in the data industry.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s ideals—modularity, open source, and avoiding lock-in—are less important to most buyers than consolidated, tightly integrated suites that simply work. Microsoft’s dominance in BI and databases shows that customers often prefer convenience, bundling, and sales reach over craft and composability. The likely future, he suggests, is consolidation, not a sprawling best‑of‑breed ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: For most data buyers, the practical benefits of a consolidated, integrated suite outweigh the ideals of modularity and openness, making consolidation the more realistic winning strategy.
