quotes.quote: There is a plain and obvious problem with Elon Musk owning Twitter: One man will have taken complete control of the major artery of mass political communication and elite discourse.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Elon Musk’s $265B fortune is so large that normal comparisons fail, so he offers vivid, often absurd benchmarks to make the scale legible. He then pivots to the real concern: a single private citizen controlling a major channel of public discourse is dangerous regardless of who that person is, and supporting the deal often boils down to personal affinity for Musk rather than principle.
quotes.key_insight: Musk’s fortune is nearly incomprehensible, but the more important issue is that such wealth enables one person to control a core public communications platform, a power that should be off-limits regardless of who holds it.

quotes.quote: Some things—nuclear weapons, access to the pictures on every iPhone on the planet, the entire world’s Chrome browsing history, Gmail’s servers—shouldn’t be for sale.

He then pivots to the real concern: a single private citizen controlling a major channel of public discourse is dangerous regardless of who that person is, and supporting the deal often boils down to personal affinity for Musk rather than principle.
quotes.key_insight: Musk’s fortune is nearly incomprehensible, but the more important issue is that such wealth enables one person to control a core public communications platform, a power that should be off-limits regardless of who holds it.

quotes.quote: There are only two answers. You can either support people’s right to spend money how they please—you know, free speech—and accept the anonymous purchase, or you can object to it, on general principle, because no one person should be entrusted with that power, no matter how many lotteries they win.

He then pivots to the real concern: a single private citizen controlling a major channel of public discourse is dangerous regardless of who that person is, and supporting the deal often boils down to personal affinity for Musk rather than principle.
quotes.key_insight: Musk’s fortune is nearly incomprehensible, but the more important issue is that such wealth enables one person to control a core public communications platform, a power that should be off-limits regardless of who holds it.

quotes.quote: The only other option is to just admit that you like Elon Musk, that you like the idea of him being in charge, and that nearly everything else is a rationalization for rooting for your guy.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Elon Musk’s $265B fortune is so large that normal comparisons fail, so he offers vivid, often absurd benchmarks to make the scale legible. He then pivots to the real concern: a single private citizen controlling a major channel of public discourse is dangerous regardless of who that person is, and supporting the deal often boils down to personal affinity for Musk rather than principle.
quotes.key_insight: Musk’s fortune is nearly incomprehensible, but the more important issue is that such wealth enables one person to control a core public communications platform, a power that should be off-limits regardless of who holds it.

quotes.quote: Most efforts to explain it are absurd “comparisons”—Elon Musk is worth more than all but 25 companies on the S&P 500! If you took 265 billion one dollar bills and laid them end-to-end, they’d stretch from here to Venus!—that are no easier to grasp than the original figure.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Elon Musk’s $265B fortune is so large that normal comparisons fail, so he offers vivid, often absurd benchmarks to make the scale legible. quotes.key_insight: Musk’s fortune is nearly incomprehensible, but the more important issue is that such wealth enables one person to control a core public communications platform, a power that should be off-limits regardless of who holds it.

quotes.quote: People can spin stories simply by telling us what to think.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the more common way statistics mislead isn’t through doctored charts but through the words that frame them. Adjectives like “narrowly,” “nearly perfect,” or “spiked” interpret data for us, so we accept a narrative we might not reach on the numbers alone.
quotes.key_insight: Most statistical deception happens in the narrative framing, where words interpret numbers for us and steer our conclusions.

quotes.quote: It’s not the data that gets us, but the adjectives that describe it.

quotes.quote: Perhaps, to nail a disputation on the church door, one of the core responsibilities of a data team—modeling a business, and defining a semantic layer—is best done in another language.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that SQL’s dominance has encouraged data teams to model business logic directly in queries, but many real business processes aren’t relational and get poorly approximated as a result. He suggests that semantic layers should move beyond pure SQL, via richer logic languages (like Jinja-heavy dbt or Malloy) or even non-SQL databases, to capture complex business models more faithfully.
quotes.key_insight: SQL-centric semantic layers make business modeling accessible but brittle, and richer logic-driven layers may be necessary to represent complex operations accurately.

quotes.quote: This type of semantic layer has its problems, though. Most notably, there is notrue model.

quotes.quote: In other words, defining a semantic layer in pure SQL is like animating a movie by hand.

quotes.quote: Which leads us to the heretical conclusion: Just as SQL can, but shouldn’t, be used to define complex statistical analysis, perhaps SQL can, but probably shouldn’t, be used to model the complex operations of a business.

quotes.quote: Databricks is a $38 billion dollar mistake.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the Big Data era is ending not because data got less important, but because the industry finally shifted from grand, hype-driven promises to boring, practical tools like Snowflake and dbt. The real breakthrough is lowering friction and cost so “ordinary” teams can do more with familiar workflows, which will quietly unlock new use cases over time. He contends Databricks and similar tools should stop selling visionary narratives and instead sell themselves as simple, better databases.
quotes.key_insight: Big Data’s promise is being realized not through revolutionary new paradigms, but through boring, reliable infrastructure that makes familiar analytics cheaper and easier.

quotes.quote: But we never connected these dots during our evaluation because the story Databricks told was buzzwords.

quotes.quote: We have to bring other people back into our conversations.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that writers and data professionals inevitably tailor what they say to the audience they want to impress, often defaulting to an insular circle of industry peers and influencers. This inward focus distorts priorities in the data community—conference talk, tool debates, and discourse drift toward self-referential validation rather than serving end users. The remedy is to deliberately broaden the audience we write and speak for, seeking respect from people outside the data bubble.
quotes.key_insight: The real problem isn’t what the data community talks about but the narrow audience it talks to, and widening that audience is the only way to regain perspective on what actually matters.

quotes.quote: The point I’d hoped to make was that it’s not the content of what we talk about that matters, but who we make that content for.

quotes.quote: OLAP cubes are just tables, but tables structured in a very particular way.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that OLAP cubes persist as a conceptual “ghost” in modern BI tools: even when the infrastructure no longer requires cubes, interfaces still expose confusing, cube-like abstractions. He contends that BI should hide internal data structure and instead present data in the simple forms users actually think in—metrics and flat tables—so tools are understandable without implicit knowledge.
quotes.key_insight: Even as OLAP cubes fade technically, BI tools still mirror their confusing abstractions, so we should redesign interfaces around how users ask questions—metrics and flat tables—rather than how data is modeled.

quotes.quote: But there’s another, much less discussed downside to OLAP cubes: They’re very difficult to understand.

quotes.quote: Revenue is a one-way door—once you use it as the scale with which you weigh yourself, you never step off.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that while revenue eventually matters, early-stage startups should resist making it their defining KPI, especially during market downturns. Using revenue as the main yardstick distorts incentives and alignment; better, durable metrics help teams focus on long-term value creation rather than short-term extraction. Data teams, in particular, should champion alternative north stars that are singular, reachable, and enduring.
quotes.key_insight: Revenue is inevitable, but making it the primary KPI too early warps incentives and focus, so startups—especially data teams—should elevate clearer, longer-term metrics as their north star.

quotes.quote: Any chart that’s sufficiently up and to the right can tell a compelling narrative, and fund a business to keep building.

quotes.quote: Acquisitions don’t create revolutions; they capture those that have already happened, and repackage them into a limited enterprise offering.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Snowflake’s acquisition of Streamlit is the start of a long-term shift toward a true data app platform, not just another top-of-stack SaaS play. He contends that a Streamlit framework plus a Snowflake-backed app marketplace could make data apps cheaper to build and sell, catalyzing a broad ecosystem of small, specialized tools built directly on the data layer.
quotes.key_insight: Snowflake’s Streamlit deal is less about a product and more about seeding a platform-plus-marketplace that could make data apps scalable to build, distribute, and monetize.

quotes.quote: Streamlit will never step foot in Gartner's data apps magic quadrant, or, I suspect, ever even attempt to bring a traditional product to market.

quotes.quote: Analysis, even SQL-based analysis, isn’t like design, where a handful of people create stuff and everyone else is a consumer, with clear lines between them.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues the real shift in the data world isn’t bundling vs. unbundling of tools, but the blurring of roles between analysts and everyone else. Based on Mode’s usage, analytical behavior spreads across functions and deepens over time, making analysis a team sport. The implication is that “modern data experience” should serve a future where many more people want to work like analysts, not just unify tooling.
quotes.key_insight: The crucial change in data work is the fading divide between analysts and everyone else, so tools should be built for broadly shared analytical behavior rather than narrow specialist roles.

quotes.quote: For data people, it's tempting to treat numbers like a universal cipher that grants you the privilege to weigh in on any issue, if only you show your cute little proof.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that in moments of clear moral crisis, the pressure to add commentary or analysis is misplaced; sometimes the right response is to acknowledge the obvious and refrain from performative insight. He emphasizes that numbers, clever takes, or detached cynicism aren’t required, and it’s acceptable to feel helpless, speak plainly, or stay quiet.
quotes.key_insight: In the face of clear-cut moral horror, the most honest response may be to admit there’s nothing clever to add and to speak (or stay silent) plainly.

quotes.quote: The lesson I’m trying to learn, while watching a confusing war on a distant shore, is that it’s ok to be at a loss for clever words (a lesson that, given the paradoxical existence of this post, I’ve yet to fully embrace).

quotes.quote: It’s ok to simply reaffirm the obvious—that, while, most conflicts are complex and nuanced, this one is not; there is nothing to add up; there are good guys and bad guys, and this is a war of unhinged aggression.

quotes.quote: Without more transparency into who’s financially aligned with whom, this risks something that should be bigger than any individual’s portfolio: the health of the community.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that undisclosed angel investing creates hidden conflicts of interest in the data community, distorting discourse and reinforcing insider cliques. The same transparency norms applied to vendors should apply to investors, because credibility in a tight-knit ecosystem depends on openly disclosing financial ties.
quotes.key_insight: The data community’s trust depends on treating angel investing like any other conflict of interest and disclosing it openly.

quotes.quote: We’re invisible thumbs on scale, gently tiling honest conversations in our financial favor.

quotes.quote: In my view, Excel is an unqualified triumph, for one very particular reason: It’s given more than a billion people direct access to the profound power of computers.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Excel is software’s greatest achievement because it turned hundreds of millions of non-programmers into programmers by making simple automation accessible. He proposes a more general “Excel for everything” that lowers the prerequisites of scripting, letting people automate any task through a block-based, spreadsheet-like interface, and reframing programming as a universal tool rather than a career identity.
quotes.key_insight: Excel’s genius is its interface for simple code, and a similarly accessible, block-based scripting tool could democratize automation far beyond spreadsheets.

quotes.quote: But the front quarter of the ship—analytics and BI tools for data consumption—the front is a very different story.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that while the back of the modern data stack has converged on clear architectural norms, the consumption layer (analytics/BI) is fragmented and unsettled. This diversity reflects real differences in people and business use cases, yet the industry still lacks shared standards and is muddied by vendor incentives. He expects eventual consolidation, driven by community consensus or economic gravity, even if innovation thrives in today’s chaos.
quotes.key_insight: The analytics consumption layer is fragmented because human and business needs vary, but it will likely consolidate toward shared standards as economic and community forces reassert order.

quotes.quote: Production is what’s in people’s heads.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data teams aren’t service organizations, but they face similar pressures and can learn from support teams’ habits. By treating stakeholders’ perceptions as the reality to understand, prioritizing empathy over cleverness, and valuing maintenance, data teams can be more effective and resilient. The core shift is from consultant-as-expert to detective-and-therapist who helps people feel heard and move forward.
quotes.key_insight: Data teams do their best work when they adopt a service mindset: listen deeply, prioritize stakeholder reality, and take pride in ongoing maintenance rather than only big projects.

quotes.quote: The overwhelming conclusion that they tell us is this: You shouldn’t lose.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses a dataset of Wordle scores shared on Twitter to show that most players win easily and that "hard mode"—reusing known greens—actually performs better than more exploratory strategies. He then pivots to the bigger lesson: data analysis is shaped by the questions we choose and the paths we ignore, so truth-telling requires acknowledging those biases.
quotes.key_insight: Data can illuminate optimal play in Wordle, but the deeper takeaway is that analysis is never fully objective because the questions we ask—and skip—shape the truths we tell.

quotes.quote: Standards that are only standards until a better idea comes along aren’t really standards at all.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data startups overhype “open standards” as the modern equivalent of network effects, but most so‑called standards are fragile and transient. Durable success usually comes from building useful products and brands, not from winning imaginary winner‑take‑all markets. He proposes a practical, paid “switchboard” for metadata plumbing rather than another aspirational standard.
quotes.key_insight: In the data stack, chasing open standards is mostly a pitch narrative; real value and money come from pragmatic products that solve integration plumbing without relying on network effects.

quotes.quote: The modern data stack—and more specifically, the centralized data warehouse—is a good foundation; data apps are a cool idea.
quotes.summary: Benn argues that today’s warehouse-centered modern data stack can’t really support a thriving ecosystem of data apps, and reverse ETL is a brittle stopgap rather than a true platform. He proposes an “entity layer”—a generalized, interoperable semantic layer for business objects—as the missing bridge that could make data apps reliable and eventually turn the warehouse into an operational brain.
quotes.key_insight: An entity layer that standardizes business objects—beyond metrics—could be the missing abstraction that turns the warehouse into a true platform for data apps.

quotes.quote: But as platforms for backing data apps, they’re a fudge.

quotes.quote: The magic of TikTok’s medium is that it’s built around stories, not takes.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that blogging should be treated less like Twitter’s contrarian take economy and more like TikTok’s remixable, story-driven culture, where familiar ideas can be retold with fresh perspective. He shares a candid, iterative writing process—freeform conversation first, structure later—to help hesitant writers start and keep momentum, emphasizing that authenticity and enjoyment matter more than polish.
quotes.key_insight: Sustainable, enjoyable writing comes from conversational drafting and remixing familiar ideas rather than chasing originality or rigid outlines.

quotes.quote: Rather than chase new ideas, or feel the pressure to make everything original, I try to accept that everything’s already been said.

quotes.quote: Semantically anomalous data is structured correctly—it has the right schema, strings are indeed strings—and is statistically uninteresting—it’s an acceptable number of records, and doesn’t include a row for a transaction of negative eighty trillion dollars—but is still wrong.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that modern data observability catches structural and statistical issues but misses the most dangerous failures: semantic anomalies where data is technically valid yet conceptually wrong. These errors evade alerts, erode trust, and can drive major business mistakes, so teams need tools and practices that directly encode business meaning and keep humans in the loop.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest risks in analytics come from semantic errors—data that looks fine but means the wrong thing—so observability must evolve to encode business meaning and reinforce human context, not just monitor pipelines.

quotes.quote: It’s data that, on the trip from measurement to meaning, got lost in translation.

quotes.quote: The analytics community’s success is proof that, even if software eats the world, software culture doesn’t have to.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the early-2010s tech culture he experienced was not a temporary excess but a symptom of deeper structural incentives that breed toxicity. In contrast, the analytics community—exemplified by dbt’s Slack and Coalesce—has built a healthier, more inclusive culture, proving that tech’s ambition and impact can be separated from its worst behaviors. He concludes that software should learn from analytics by exporting this cultural model outward into the broader industry.
quotes.key_insight: The analytics community demonstrates that tech’s cultural toxicity is not inevitable, and its healthier norms should be exported back into the broader software industry.

quotes.quote: It’s proof that the good things about the tech industry—the ambition, the impact, the financial opportunity—aren’t the causes of the bad.

quotes.quote: Data and metrics—revenues, retention rates, product usage patterns—don’t measure a company’s world; they are its world.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that dashboards and reporting aren’t distractions from “real” analytics work; they are how companies construct a shared reality in an abstract, non-physical world. Metrics don’t just measure the business—they define it—so consistent dashboards are foundational for trust and decision-making.
quotes.key_insight: Dashboards are essential because they create the consistent, shared reality that companies need to operate in an abstract world.

quotes.quote: The code is the concept, and the concept is the code.

quotes.quote: My argument is simple: It’s called coffee cake.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues, half-seriously and with mock rigor, that the name “coffee cake” is a linguistic fraud because the cake contains no coffee. He marshals examples and a pseudo-statistical survey of cake names to claim the exception is absurd and should be corrected by renaming it “coffree cake.”
quotes.key_insight: The post uses mock data analysis and linguistic pedantry to argue that “coffee cake” is a misleading exception that should be renamed.

quotes.quote: Coffee cake, I’m told, is a reasonable exception because you eat coffee cake with coffee—suggesting, I suppose, that potato salad is a salad eaten with potatoes, or espresso martinis are regular martinis for chasing your morning coffee, or maple syrup is a sugary topping for trees.

quotes.quote: The terminal point of an analyst’s career, it seems, is the data leader treadmill, hopping between different VP roles, always running but never moving forward.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analytics leaders get stuck on a career treadmill just below the C-suite because the chief data/analytics role is defined as an operational, second-class position. He proposes splitting the function like engineering does: keep a VP of data for management and create a chief analytics officer who sits with executives to do strategic, high-leverage analysis. This would improve executive decision-making while giving strong analysts a real top-level career path.
quotes.key_insight: The analytics function needs a true executive-level analyst role—peer to the CTO—to unlock strategic impact and provide a viable top-tier career path for analysts.

quotes.quote: Decision makers, from CEOs to the person on the couch with the remote, also use data to serve a more subtle purpose: As a substitute for courage.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that data and analysis are often used not to clarify decisions but to escape them, letting leaders off the hook when choices are hard and evidence is inconclusive. He contends that real leadership requires courage to decide and own outcomes when the data cannot provide certainty, and analysts must recognize when their role is to inform rather than to defer responsibility.
quotes.key_insight: Data should inform decisions, but when evidence is inconclusive, leaders must choose with conviction rather than hide behind analysis.

quotes.quote: Analysis like this, that’s chasing a conclusive result for its own sake, is a cover for surreptitiously handing decisions over to chance.

quotes.quote: Analysts should judge their work by how quickly people make decisions with it.
quotes.summary: The post argues that analytics lacks a reliable way to measure itself, leading to circular judgments, bias, and poor incentives. Benn proposes a concrete metric: evaluate analysts by how quickly their work helps others make decisions, because that directly reflects the purpose of analysis and improves communication, focus, and reputation over time.
quotes.key_insight: The best way to evaluate analytical work is the speed with which it enables sound decisions.

quotes.quote: In its normal operation, costs are intertwined and opaque.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s fragmentation hides compounding costs: each new tool and higher-fidelity use case triggers cascading usage fees across vendors. Because data tooling is mostly services-oriented and priced inconsistently, the ecosystem is economically unstable unless it consolidates or builds better coordinating layers. The likely outcome is fewer dominant platforms with satellite products and stronger cost management.
quotes.key_insight: The modern data stack’s economic problem isn’t demand but the fractured, opaque, usage-amplifying supply that makes costs balloon unless the market consolidates or coordinates.

quotes.quote: We just did it, all of us, on our own.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that consumer products like Yelp made data-driven decisions feel natural by embedding data inside the experience, while corporate analytics isolates data in dashboards divorced from work. The future of operational analytics is to integrate focused, disciplined data experiences directly into workflows and build a data “operating system” that enables those experiences rather than just more BI tooling.
quotes.key_insight: Operational analytics will only become ubiquitous when data is embedded seamlessly into the tools and decisions people already use, not isolated in standalone dashboards.

quotes.quote: These services don’t attempt to make you “data-driven.” Instead, they focus on a bigger problem—choosing a restaurant, a means of transit, and a reservation booking—and integrate data alongside other features, like phone numbers, pictures of food, and links to menu, that help you address it.

quotes.quote: Looker’s crown jewel is LookML; Tableau’s is visualization.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the Looker–Tableau integration signals a structural split in BI: governance/modeling (LookML) is separating from visualization and exploration. This shift redefines BI away from all-in-one suites toward a metrics/governance layer paired with best-in-class exploration tools, with big implications for vendor consolidation and the economics of the modern data stack.
quotes.key_insight: The Looker–Tableau integration marks a decisive unbundling of BI, with governance/metrics becoming a separate layer and exploration tools defining the new BI landscape.

quotes.quote: In the middle of the analytical field, in the eye of our swirling hurricane about titles and team structures and tooling, is analysis itself.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the public Facebook leak revealed something rare: not just debate about social media, but about the quality of analysis itself. Because most analysis is proprietary, the field obsesses over tools and structures while the actual work stays invisible, making it hard to define or improve what “good analysis” looks like. He concludes that quality is subjective and multi-dimensional, and the way forward is to expose more real analysis so standards can evolve.
quotes.key_insight: Good analysis can’t be reduced to a single yardstick, and without public examples of real work, the field can’t reliably raise its standards.

quotes.quote: The analysis we produce is still only in our peripheral vision, at the center a black hole: unknowable on its own, only detectable by its effects.

quotes.quote: Visualizations are the ultimate cookie.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that BI and visualization are far more complex than feature backlogs suggest, so “thin” consumption apps coordinated by a data OS are unlikely to deliver a coherent user experience. He proposes a middle path: a strong, general-purpose BI “planet” for exploration, surrounded by specialized satellites for specific use cases.
quotes.key_insight: Because visualization and BI are inherently complex experience layers, a single general-purpose “planet” tool with specialized satellites is more realistic than a fully decentralized ecosystem of thin apps.

quotes.quote: We should ship an experience that mirrors what people want to do, not one that mirrors the org charts of the teams helping them do it.

quotes.quote: This shows the limits of self-perceived kindness and progressivism.
quotes.summary: The post argues that the analytics community has built a reputation for being welcoming, but that welcome is not shared equally, with Black professionals profoundly underrepresented in conferences and the field. The author contends that diversity should be pursued as a moral imperative, not a business case, and that the community’s blind spots risk embedding and amplifying racism through data-driven systems.
quotes.key_insight: A community can be outwardly kind yet structurally exclusionary, and in data this exclusion risks hard-coding racial bias into systems and careers.

quotes.quote: The data industry has the money to do more. It remains to be seen if it has the commitment.

The author contends that diversity should be pursued as a moral imperative, not a business case, and that the community’s blind spots risk embedding and amplifying racism through data-driven systems.
quotes.key_insight: A community can be outwardly kind yet structurally exclusionary, and in data this exclusion risks hard-coding racial bias into systems and careers.

quotes.quote: It’s as though we took our definition of BI from twenty years ago and started crossing off clauses, until we’re left with “visualization and reporting.”
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that BI, like early software, has been unbundled and stripped of legacy connotations, leaving today’s BI as a diminished fragment focused on visualization and reporting. He contends the next generation of BI should be a universal consumption layer that integrates self-serve and deep analysis, sits atop shared governance, and centers collaboration between analysts and non-analysts.
quotes.key_insight: BI should evolve from a fragmented, report-centric tool into a universal data-consumption platform that unifies self-serve and deep analysis while relying on shared governance layers.

quotes.quote: BI tools should aspire to do one thing, and do it completely: They should be the universal tool for people to consume and make sense of data.

quotes.quote: Data is not the level ground that we act like it is.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that just as the Supreme Court’s rulings are shaped by human preferences and political context, data-driven arguments are similarly tilted by subjective choices and prior beliefs. Data feels like neutral ground, but the metrics and analyses we treat as objective are built from arbitrary definitions, incentives, and power—so numbers often mask opinions rather than replace them. The remedy isn’t more data, but more humility about our limits and more skepticism toward quantitative authority.
quotes.key_insight: Data-driven argumentation is often treated as objective, but it is built on subjective choices and can be wielded to launder opinions as neutrality.

quotes.quote: In short, everything can be tilted: the data, the analysis, and the people analyzing it.

quotes.quote: The data ecosystem functions quite differently. Buy a new tool, and you’ve got to shove others aside, jerry-rig some tenuous connections to the rest of the stack, and hope that the whole haphazard contraption survives long enough for you to band-aid it with the next product you stuff into it.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the analytics ecosystem’s core problem isn’t just “too many tools” but the lack of an operating system that standardizes how tools integrate and behave. He critiques extreme centralization and decentralization (including the data mesh as commonly defined) and proposes a delegated, OS-like layer—potentially embodied by dbt—that abstracts heterogenous data sources and provides shared utilities for the stack.
quotes.key_insight: The analytics stack needs an OS-like layer that standardizes integrations and utilities across tools, and a delegated model like an expanded dbt could provide that foundation better than pure centralization or decentralization.

quotes.quote: In many ways, this is the price of success.

quotes.quote: Inspiration will visit you when she chooses.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that creative work has a generative phase that cannot be scheduled the way editorial work can, and meetings are especially destructive to that fragile state. By treating meetings as fixed and creativity as the flexible block, organizations stretch creative tasks into slow, demoralizing slogs. He proposes rebalancing calendars to allow people to decline meetings when in generative flow, even in data roles.
quotes.key_insight: Treating meetings as immovable and creativity as schedulable turns generative work into a slow, demoralizing grind, so calendars should flex to protect creative flow.

quotes.quote: Meetings are catastrophic when you’re searching for this spark.

quotes.quote: Until we can serve them that, our technology, no matter how revolutionary, is academic.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that while the modern data stack has delivered dramatic technical advances, it has failed to produce a coherent, reliable experience for everyday analysts and business users. The core challenge is not more tools or abstract “culture,” but designing a modern data experience that integrates workflows, trust, and collaboration across a decentralized ecosystem.
quotes.key_insight: The modern data stack’s technical progress won’t deliver its promised value until the ecosystem aligns around a cohesive, user-centered data experience.

quotes.quote: Fault lines between products become fault lines in how it feels to use the modern data stack—and for most people, that’s what matters most.

quotes.quote: The artificial boundaries we define eventually come to define us.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is carved up by an implicit technical vs. non-technical divide, which is a false boundary imposed on a continuous spectrum of work. This gerrymandering distorts product design—especially in analytics consumption—by forcing tools into narrow buckets instead of enabling a unified experience across users and use cases.
quotes.key_insight: Treating data work as a technical/non-technical dichotomy gerrymanders the stack and blocks the unified, spectrum-based tools teams actually need.

quotes.quote: The line between BI reporting and analytical research isn’t a hard line; it’s a fluid, shifting continuum.

quotes.quote: Like a city divided by a poorly drawn political boundary, our method for consuming data is split by an arbitrary technical line that makes it extraordinarily difficult to cross a border that we frequently need to cross.

quotes.quote: We analysts, I fear, are honeydews.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analytics is often seen as undervalued not just because of mislabeling or bad tooling, but because the work itself is genuinely hard and average results can be underwhelming. The field’s value depends on exceptional analysts, yet most teams can’t consistently produce that level of impact, so organizations may be reacting rationally. He suggests improving selection and cultivation—hiring for the right mix of skills and training the squishier parts of the job—to close the gap.
quotes.key_insight: Analytics is hard to do well at scale, so boosting its perceived value requires better hiring and training, not just better tools or organizational sympathy.

quotes.quote: The average engineer, by contrast, can regularly deliver more valuable results—the consistent cantaloupe to our erratic honeydew.

quotes.quote: Analytics isn’t primarily technical.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analytics is being miscast as a primarily technical, engineering-adjacent discipline, which both misidentifies what makes great analysts and excludes strong candidates who lack conventional technical credentials. He says the real differentiator is curiosity, judgment, and communication, and that the emerging analytics engineer role could either enable this distinction or blur it further.
quotes.key_insight: Analytics should be judged by curiosity, reasoning, and communication rather than by engineering credentials, or the field will keep excluding many of its best potential practitioners.

quotes.quote: While technical skills are useful, they’re not what separate average analysts from great ones.

quotes.quote: Given the choice, I’d hire Sherlock Holmes—the antiquated Sherlock Holmes, from the 19th century, a man for whom “technology” means the light bulb—over any present-day MIT engineering PhD.

quotes.quote: By describing analysts as engineers, we’re telling people we want them to solve Jamie’s problems, and then expect them to solve Erik’s—because Erik’s are the problems we really have.

quotes.quote: Defining self-serve isn’t about knowing when we see it, but knowing when we feel it.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that “self-serve analytics” isn’t defined by features or who/what answers questions, but by how the experience feels to the people using and providing it. Because trust, comfort, and skill differ by user and context, the same tool can feel self-serve to some and not to others, so teams should design for the experience that makes their users feel at home rather than chase a canonical feature checklist.
quotes.key_insight: Self-serve analytics succeeds when users feel comfortable and trust the experience, not when a tool checks a feature list.

quotes.quote: In close games, a poorly performing team wins 57 percent of the time; a well performing team wins only 43 percent of the time.
quotes.summary: Stancil analyzes 12 years of NBA games to answer how fans should feel in close games at halftime. The data shows that when the score is tied or within one point, teams that played poorly in the first half are actually more likely to win, indicating mean reversion beats momentum.
quotes.key_insight: In tight games, poor first-half performance is a good sign because teams tend to revert toward their average in the second half.

quotes.quote: In other words, mean reversion overpowers momentum (if momentum exists at all).

quotes.key_insight: In tight games, poor first-half performance is a good sign because teams tend to revert toward their average in the second half.

quotes.quote: Teams that perform great in the first half are just as likely to perform badly in the second half as they are to perform great again.

quotes.key_insight: In tight games, poor first-half performance is a good sign because teams tend to revert toward their average in the second half.

quotes.quote: If your team is in a tight game, the worse they’ve played, the better you should feel.

quotes.quote: We invest in the ephemeral, and throw away the enduring.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analytics teams overinvest in dashboards and BI systems that produce fleeting value while neglecting the ad hoc analyses that drive the most important decisions. The real problem is that these analyses are ungoverned, hard to find later, and treated as disposable. He proposes reframing and organizing this work—starting by renaming it—to make it central, durable, and systematized.
quotes.key_insight: Dashboards are a seductive but low‑durability investment; the real value comes from ad hoc analysis, which needs to be renamed, systematized, and preserved to build lasting organizational knowledge.

quotes.quote: No amount of “reimagining the deck chair” will keep our Titanic afloat.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analytics teams overinvest in dashboards and BI systems that produce fleeting value while neglecting the ad hoc analyses that drive the most important decisions. The real problem is that these analyses are ungoverned, hard to find later, and treated as disposable. He proposes reframing and organizing this work—starting by renaming it—to make it central, durable, and systematized.
quotes.key_insight: Dashboards are a seductive but low‑durability investment; the real value comes from ad hoc analysis, which needs to be renamed, systematized, and preserved to build lasting organizational knowledge.

quotes.quote: By naming it something temporary, we make it temporary.

quotes.quote: Though analogies may seem like misdirection, I believe they’re actually clarifying—even when they don’t seem to make sense.
quotes.summary: Benn argues that analogies aren’t obfuscation but a clarifying tool because they force us to simplify a complex idea into its essential structure before comparing it to something else. That simplification exposes the logical core and prevents us from hiding behind jargon or unnecessary complexity. He links this directly to analytics work, where the hardest task is distilling signal from noise and telling a clear, memorable story.
quotes.key_insight: Analogies work because the act of simplifying a complex idea into its essential structure is what clarifies both analysis and communication.

quotes.quote: To create an analogy, you have to pull out the essential bones of an idea, and map them onto the bones of another idea.

quotes.quote: When we use simple models, we can’t hide behind complexity or unnecessary details, either intentionally or inadvertently, to obscure our argument's logical core.

quotes.quote: What’s the best way to format these parts of a query? The answer—an answer every analyst should love, if not many priests—is that it depends.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that strict, uniform SQL formatting rules are less important than making a query’s logic legible to humans. Formatting should adapt to the structure and emphasis of the logic, even if that breaks conventional style guides or what a formatter can automate.
quotes.key_insight: Effective SQL formatting is not about rigid rules but about highlighting the logic that matters, even if that means breaking conventions.

quotes.quote: Queries—which, above all, transcribe vague business concepts, like revenue and win rate, into precise formulae—should also make their logic legible.

quotes.quote: The bespoke elements of our Salesforce data that distinguish it from that of other B2B SaaS businesses aren’t analytical blemishes to be polished off of a template that just sums the “amount” field on every Salesforce opportunity.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that despite increasing standardization in data stacks, reusable dashboard templates haven’t taken off because the most meaningful business data differences are intentional and central to how each company operates. Even when tooling and schemas align, companies define success differently, making one-size-fits-all reporting impractical; at best, templates might work in narrow, rigid domains if they ask for meaning-first inputs.
quotes.key_insight: Standardized tools don’t yield standardized meaning, so templates fail because the differences that matter most are the ones businesses intentionally encode in their data and metrics.

quotes.quote: In other words, the shared foundation that templates are supposed to be built on is often only name deep.

quotes.quote: Self-serve analytics, which promises to open up vast troves of “insight” by making data accessible to everyone at a company, doesn’t actually work.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that “self‑serve analytics is a mirage” has become a fashionable shibboleth, but the real issue is that analysts project their own needs onto business users. When you define self‑serve narrowly—mostly as consistent metric extraction—it becomes solvable, while deeper analysis still requires hiring more analytical thinkers.
quotes.key_insight: Self‑serve fails when treated as analyst‑grade exploration, but works when scoped to standardized metric extraction, while truly complex questions still demand more analysts.

quotes.quote: The problem self-serve is meant to solve—that there are more questions than analysts, and we don’t have time to answer them all—is still a problem, even if self-serve is a lousy solution.

quotes.quote: These are the unholy creatures who, upon finding their seat, take a couple glances at the bustling tarmac outside, and push the window shade shut, like a pedestrian waving away a Greenpeace canvasser.
quotes.summary: The post argues that closing airplane window shades is a small but corrosive act of selfishness that traps everyone in a sterile, joyless cabin. Stancil frames the window as a hard-won, almost sacred portal to wonder, and implores the window-seat passenger to share that view rather than retreat into screens.
quotes.key_insight: Closing the window shade symbolizes modern complacency with screens and convenience, squandering a rare, communal chance to experience awe.

quotes.quote: To them, the tiny perforations in our barrel of steel and precious life aren't an engineering marvel, or a portal to the divine horizon through which were hurtling, or even a way to get a little bit of pleasing natural light in humming, sterile tube; no, they are irritants to be extinguished, distracting from the more important business on their screens.

quotes.quote: Data isn’t objective, and analysis isn’t structured. It’s just as creative—and just as messy—as the papers I ran away from in college.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that analytics is inherently creative and messy, not an objective, linear path to truth. Metrics are subjective constructs shaped by business context, so early analytical work should embrace exploration before converging on stable definitions. Attempts to prematurely impose structure or swap tools often mistake the natural mess of discovery for failure.
quotes.key_insight: Analytics work succeeds when teams accept that defining metrics is a creative, messy process and plan to refine it later rather than forcing premature structure.

quotes.quote: There is no correct win rate waiting to be unearthed; one version isn’t true while another is false.

quotes.quote: Closeness to someone doesn't make us know them better—it makes us take their side, for better and for worse.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that proximity doesn’t just humanize people—it biases us in their favor, often excusing real faults. He uses Paul Graham’s defense of Antonio García Martínez to show how personal closeness clouds judgment and leads to asymmetric scrutiny. The piece concludes that public accountability matters because elites tend to protect their own.
quotes.key_insight: Personal closeness often distorts judgment in favor of the powerful, making public accountability essential to counter elite self-protection.

quotes.quote: We have the tools for governing tables, and we have tools for governing dashboards and analysis, but we’re still pretty sloppy about governing the space between the two.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Airbnb’s Minerva addresses the core problem in analytics: inconsistent metric definitions across teams and tools. He praises Minerva’s metrics layer and its integration with the data catalog for shifting discovery toward metric-centric, consumer-friendly views. His main critique is the API-first access, which makes adoption and SQL-centric analyst workflows harder than a warehouse-native approach.
quotes.key_insight: A metrics layer solves a real governance gap, but it should integrate seamlessly with SQL-centric workflows and existing tools to be broadly adoptable.

quotes.quote: In the middle of a sea of brown and beige heads, yellow stood out like a data scientist on a TV panel of seasoned political anchors.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that if you want a Twitter profile picture that truly stands out, you should treat it as a color-contrast optimization problem rather than a flattering headshot. By sampling thousands of avatars and comparing dominant hues, he shows that extreme, edge-of-the-wheel colors maximize visual distinctiveness against both other profiles and Twitter’s UI.
quotes.key_insight: A purely attention-optimizing Twitter avatar is best achieved by choosing a highly contrasting, extreme color rather than a conventional photo.

quotes.quote: Except for one: a metrics layer.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is missing a crucial component: a centralized metrics layer that defines and serves consistent metric logic across all tools. Without it, self-serve analytics devolves into fragmented, inconsistent calculations, and BI tools end up as isolated silos rather than shared infrastructure. A proper metrics layer would make metrics universally accessible via SQL, unify reporting with deeper analysis, and complete the stack’s foundation.
quotes.key_insight: A centralized, SQL-addressable metrics layer is the missing foundation needed to make self-serve analytics consistent across the modern data stack.

quotes.key_insight: A centralized, SQL-addressable metrics layer is the missing foundation needed to make self-serve analytics consistent across the modern data stack.

quotes.quote: A better architecture would do for metrics what dbt did for transformed data—make them globally accessible to every other tool in the data stack.

quotes.quote: In response to these changes, companies are turning their stacks sideways.
quotes.summary: Benn argues that the data tooling industry is shifting from vertically integrated, all-in-one products to a horizontally integrated “stack” where each layer (ingestion, storage, transformation, consumption) is owned by specialized tools. This change is driven by the explosion of vendors and the need to blend data across functions, but it creates new challenges around integration and boundary discipline.
quotes.key_insight: The modern data stack is evolving toward horizontal specialization, which improves flexibility but makes interoperability and clear architectural boundaries the central challenges.

quotes.quote: One tool is responsible for each layer—each stage of the data supply chain—across the entire business.

quotes.quote: Self-serve initiatives try to solve the wrong problem.
quotes.summary: Benn Stancil argues that self-serve BI keeps failing because teams define it as a code-free version of analyst work, which is the wrong goal. Analysts create new measures to test hypotheses, while non-analysts mostly want to extract known metrics, so self-serve should be built around opinionated “metric extraction” rather than open-ended analysis.
quotes.key_insight: Self-serve BI should focus on constrained metric extraction, not on replicating analysts’ open-ended analytical work.

quotes.quote: On August 27, I realized why data analysts like myself aren’t typically among the first employees at tech startups—you don’t have much to do.
quotes.summary: Stancil recounts how, as a data analyst cofounding a startup with no product or data, he found himself without a clear role and started a blog to explore quirky data stories. Years later, he’s relaunching that impulse on Substack, committing to regular posts that mix data culture and tech commentary with his eclectic interests.
quotes.key_insight: The Substack is a renewed, more regular outlet for Stancil’s mix of data-driven analysis and broader tech-cultural commentary, shaped by his experience building a data-focused company.

quotes.quote: In Bois’ world, Bonds finishes with an on-base percentage of .608.
quotes.summary: Stancil uses a simulation of Barry Bonds's 2004 pitches to show that Bonds could have nearly matched his historic on-base percentage without swinging, because pitchers avoided the strike zone. Extending the method league-wide suggests that, as zone rates have fallen, many hitters would actually improve their OBP by swinging less, implying modern pitching has made patience unusually valuable.
quotes.key_insight: As pitchers increasingly avoid the strike zone, the expected value of simply watching pitches rises so much that the average hitter can improve by swinging less.

quotes.quote: Bonds in isolation is breathtaking: He could’ve had the greatest OBP of all time without ever taking a single swing.

quotes.quote: The only constant is the outcome.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the missing wave of public self-flagellation after the Atlanta murders isn’t a sign of progress but evidence of how racism adapts to preserve itself. He suggests that when anti-Asian racism isn’t widely recognized as structural, there’s no safe “systemic” shield for apologies, so blame shifts and the caste system stays intact. The core point is that racism in America is flexible and self-protecting, and its contradictions make it stronger, not weaker.
quotes.key_insight: The absence of performative apologies after Atlanta reveals how racism’s power lies in its adaptability, not in consistent logic.

quotes.quote: Racism in America isn’t defined by an ordered set of principles, but is shapeshifting and infinitely flexible, able to take whatever form is necessary to perpetuate it.

quotes.quote: Graham’s number isn’t wrong—it’s irrelevant.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that Paul Graham’s widely cited math on a startup wealth tax is not wrong so much as misleading, because it relies on extreme, compounding assumptions about growth rates, time horizons, and equity outcomes. Small tweaks to those assumptions swing the results wildly, revealing that spreadsheet models in this context mostly reflect prior policy beliefs rather than hard truths.
quotes.key_insight: Wealth-tax impact estimates for startup founders hinge on extreme exponential-growth assumptions, so the headline numbers say more about the modeler’s priors than about the policy itself.

quotes.quote: Maybe that easy cynicism is exactly the problem.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the Clubhouse “slur” controversy shouldn’t be dismissed as mere performative tech-media drama, even if it looks like it. By comparing it to past journalism blowups, he suggests that Lorenz’s mistake can be read as a desperate outburst rooted in real, long-ignored harassment rather than a cynical hit job. The piece presses readers to examine the system that creates this rage instead of only condemning the person who erupts.
quotes.key_insight: The real story isn’t a single mistaken tweet but the long-ignored harassment that can drive people to desperate, attention-forcing outbursts.

quotes.quote: Maybe Lorenz’s tweet wasn’t a pantomimed punch that accidentally landed, but was a real outburst, born of furious despair.

quotes.quote: All it takes is social media, at a global scale, operating as intended.
quotes.summary: Stancil argues that the January 6th attack and the GameStop saga reveal the same structural danger: social media enables fringe groups to reach critical mass quickly, turning online narratives into real-world destabilization. Because scale and speed are the product, even well-intentioned movements can spiral, leaving society on a runaway train with only two options—submit to the ride or dismantle the track.
quotes.key_insight: The core risk isn’t misinformation or bad actors alone, but the scale of social media itself, which reliably converts fringe energy into real-world disruption.
